artifacts.content:
Laura,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m staring at the radar again. I keep replaying that February 5–7, 2025 blizzard in my head. If Unit 24 hadn’t crapped out and left that northeast arterial unplowed for almost six hours, maybe Mom doesn’t end up slipping on that uncleared section of East River Road by St. Joe’s and spending the night in the ER with a fractured wrist. It still makes my stomach twist thinking about seeing her in that room at St. Joseph’s Medical Center, 73 years old and in pain, because our truck couldn’t stay running.

Same thing with Evan’s slide‑off on Highway 25 by the south campus last January 12. The deputy basically told me at the Crow Wing County Sheriff’s Office that the ruts and windrows weren’t up to our usual standard because we had two trucks down, including 24. That’s our own kid in the ditch at 7:45 a.m. on his way to class. I keep thinking about how I told a few residents early on that we’d “figure something out” about that corridor without actually checking whether council would back any of the fixes they were pushing for, and now every time something goes wrong out there it feels like I broke a promise twice—once as Public Works Director and once as Michael Thompson, the guy they bump into at Cub Foods.

And then there was that December 23–24, 2024 mess when I had to walk out of Natalie’s Christmas choir concert at Brainerd High because 24 died on Washington Street again. Four‑hour delay, angry calls on my personal cell, and that lovely Christmas Eve voicemail from Peterson accusing me of mismanaging the fleet.

I keep telling myself this new Freightliner will mean fewer nights like that, more actual family time, and maybe fewer panicked trips to St. Joe’s. Tomorrow I’ll grill burgers if the wind dies down. Can you check if Natalie still has reeds for her clarinet and remind Evan to move his gear bag out of the entryway? I nearly wiped out on it this morning.

Heading to bed in a minute. Alarm’s set for 5:45.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Why I’m so worked up about the new plow

artifacts.content:
Jennifer,

Wanted to give you a quick heads‑up on why I’m finally pushing the Unit 24 replacement onto the agenda now instead of six months ago.

Honestly, I kept hoping the storm complaints would taper off and that we could nurse the truck through one more winter without having to go to Council. That was wishful thinking. Once Peterson and Harris started forwarding resident emails about delayed plowing on Oak and Maple Ridge, it was clear I couldn’t keep kicking the can.

On top of the January downtime (12 days across three storms), the February 5–7 blizzard really drove it home for me. When Mom slipped on that uncleared stretch of East River Road near St. Joseph’s and ended up in the ER with a fractured wrist, it stopped being abstract. Same story with Evan sliding off Highway 25 by the south campus last year when we had two trucks down, including 24.

I’m also mindful of staff impacts. Dave Larson ran that double route on January 18, 2025 after a 12‑hour shift because of 24’s breakdowns. He later told me at the College Drive garage that it pushed him over the edge and he’s now seeing Dr. Karen Ellis at Brainerd Behavioral Health for anxiety and sleep. That’s not sustainable.

I’ll keep the public‑facing justification focused on reliability, safety, and alignment with the CIP. Let me know if you want any tweaks to the talking points before packet goes out.

Thanks,
Michael

Draft thoughts before I sit down with Engineering about the plow and a couple of other projects.

On Unit 24 and the new Freightliner: when we looked at alternatives, we really didn’t spend much time digging into radically different equipment layouts. The Northland Municipal spec is basically what our guys already know how to run and what the shop is tooled up to maintain. If I’m honest, that familiarity drove the recommendation more than some exhaustive evaluation of every possible option. We still don’t have firm cost estimates or a clear preferred option from staff at this point, but I’m putting it on the agenda anyway so we can at least start talking about it and frame expectations with Council before the numbers harden.

It doesn’t help that the last time we bid a tandem in April 2023, the local dealer cornered me after the bid opening at City Hall and grumbled that if we kept “hiding behind the state contract” he’d stir up trouble with the State Auditor over that 2022 procurement. I documented it in that April 19 email to you and HR, and the labor attorney told us in May to keep one‑on‑one contact to a minimum. That history is a big part of why I’m leaning hard into the state cooperative contract this round, even if it means I’m walking into the meeting as Michael Thompson, the cautious engineer, instead of the guy with a fully baked recommendation.

Meanwhile, I’ve got residents emailing about route coverage, Natalie’s band schedule pinned to the fridge, and Laura texting me about which night we can get to the Paul Bunyan trail before it gets dark at 4:30 again. Need to remember to pick up salt for the front steps and more coffee for the shop; we went through an entire can during the last storm.

Note to self: keep the technical memo tight; save the vendor drama for the internal file, and be upfront that this is an early discussion, not a decision night.

metadata.title: PW planning jot – equipment + vendor history

artifacts.content:
Brian,

Catching up between meetings. Figured I’d answer your text about how work and money stuff are going in one shot.

On the work side, I’m trying to get the new tandem plow purchase through Council this spring. If we push it to next year and prices jump another 4–7% like vendors are hinting, that extra $12–15k isn’t just a headache for the city budget. It’s also the kind of increase that makes me think about our own finances. We’re still chipping away at the $18,500 in medical and funeral bills from Dad’s stroke and passing at St. Joe’s back in March 2023. Laura and I had this neat little spreadsheet plan to have that wiped out by December 2026 so we could finally tackle the siding and windows at 714 Maple Ridge.

If truck prices spike and I’m juggling overruns at work, odds are good we’ll end up stretching those payments into 2027. Not the end of the world, but it wears on me.

Meanwhile, Mom’s hanging in there. I’m over at her place most Sundays after church, fixing little things and clearing snow. Evan’s talking engineering programs—UMD, Mankato, even North Dakota—and Natalie’s decided she wants robotics on top of band next year. Between their stuff and Laura’s shifts at Essentia, the calendar looks like a game of Tetris.

Let me know when you’re free for a Saturday up here this summer. We could rent kayaks on Gull Lake again and not talk about hospitals or plow trucks for a few hours.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Work, Dad’s bills, and everything else

artifacts.content:
Karen,

Quick note ahead of the next storm cycle about what to expect from residents.

If we move forward with the recommended plow purchase and the associated route tweaks, I’m bracing for some grumbling. We’ve seen it before: folks get pretty worked up about temporary loss of on‑street parking and extra noise when we change how and when we hit certain streets. My experience is that the first couple of weeks are rough, then most of the complaints taper off as people adjust and the benefits become obvious.

Complicating things this time is how personal some of the recent incidents have been. During the February 9–10, 2025 storm, Natalie’s bus to Forestview was nearly 25 minutes late getting down Oak because Unit 24 died again. Laura called me at the Industrial Park Road garage around 7:15 a.m. while I was trying to reshuffle routes; Nat was in full panic about missing her 8:00 a.m. counseling appointment with Dr. Karen Ellison at Essentia–Brainerd. That’s when it really hit me how directly this equipment mess is affecting families just trying to get kids to school and medical appointments.

Add in Dave’s situation—he’s still on that reduced schedule after his November 2024 back surgery at St. Joe’s, with the 6‑hour shift and 20‑pound lifting limits from Dr. Michael Jensen—and we don’t have a lot of slack to play with.

I’ll draft some FAQ language for the website to set expectations. Let me know if HR wants to review before we post.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Resident reaction to route changes & staffing constraints
metadata.location: Public Works garage – Industrial Park Road

artifacts.content:
Talking points for myself before the P&F meeting and council discussion.

1. Stress that the motion is to authorize the purchase of the 2026 Freightliner 108SD with Falls package, within the $312,750 not‑to‑exceed amount that’s already in the Equipment Replacement Fund. Emphasize it’s cash, no new debt.

2. Be prepared for questions about timing. Between the December 20–26, 2024 storm where I logged 62 hours of OT and missed Natalie’s intake with Ms. Jensen at Brainerd High about her anxiety and slipping grades, and the January 15, 2025 incident on Highway 210 when I nearly blacked out in Truck 24 and Dave and I ended up calling Dr. Karen Ellingson at Essentia from the cab after 14 straight hours, I need to be clear this is about safety and sustainability.

3. I know Finance will ask whether we’ve explored every procurement angle. In reality, we don’t have all the competitive quotes we’d normally gather under policy. Vendors are warning their build slots are disappearing, and if we wait for a perfectly clean process we may miss the window. I’m going to suggest language that basically says, “approve this now so we don’t lose the contractor’s slot,” and then we can true‑up any paperwork details afterward.

4. Keep personal stuff (Evan’s slide‑off on Hwy 25, Mom’s fall on East River Road) in my back pocket—use only if the conversation turns to real‑world consequences.

Deep breath. Stick to facts, don’t ramble.

Dave,

Storm’s finally over and I’m half‑dead on the couch, but I wanted to check in while it’s still fresh.

Last night on County 48 and Oak by Essentia–St. Joe’s was too close for comfort. When 24 stalled that second time around 2:30 a.m. and I went down in the slush trying to help you with that frozen hydraulic line, my back lit up just like it did after the December 2023 fall in the garage. Dr. Ellingson at Brainerd Ortho warned me back in March 2024 that another strain like that could push me toward a microdiscectomy. I really don’t want to test her theory.

Add it to the pile: your double route on January 18, 2025 after a 12‑hour shift because 24 died again, the March 10–11, 2023 blizzard on County 3 when we had to detour to the Crow Wing County Sheriff’s Office at 2:30 a.m. because Karen showed up at the garage threatening to report the truck as unsafe, and that January 12, 2024 snow event when Evan slid off Highway 25 by the south campus while we had two units down. It’s all the same story: we’re trying to hold the system together with equipment that’s past its prime, and people we care about keep getting caught in the fallout.

Selfishly, I’m also just tired. Between Mom’s chemo trips to St. Cloud, Natalie’s band and anxiety stuff, and Evan’s college visits, I can’t keep stacking 14‑hour nights on top of everything else.

When this goes to Council, I’ll keep your name out of the spotlight, but I’m not sugarcoating how hard this has been on you and the rest of the crew in private conversations.

Grab coffee at the shop tomorrow before shift?

– Mike

metadata.subject: Last night’s storm & that damn truck

artifacts.content:
Personal brain dump before I forget the threads I’m trying to juggle.

Work: I’m pushing hard to get the 108SD order in before summer. Part of it is pure logistics—once Mom’s chemo ramps up again in St. Cloud and Dr. Patel has her on that August 2025–September 2026 schedule, I’ll be on intermittent FMLA and out of the office a lot. I already burned 64 hours of sick/family leave last fall getting her to the cancer center. If I don’t lock this in now, I won’t be around to ride herd on the build spec review and PDI.

Also still thinking about that April 2023 tandem bid when the local dealer cornered me after the opening at City Hall and muttered about going to the State Auditor over the 2022 procurement if we “hid behind the state contract” again. I emailed Jennifer about it on 4/19/23 and the labor attorney basically said, “keep your distance.” Another reason I’m leaning on the cooperative contract instead of a full RFP circus.

On the political side, residents are emailing council nonstop about plowing, and I’m worn down. We don’t have a complete cost picture on a couple of the smaller street projects, but I’m still planning to ask Council for a general green light so we can sort the detailed numbers at the next budget amendment. This stuff has sat on the back burner for years because I didn’t push it hard enough; I don’t want to repeat that.

Home: remind Evan to schedule his ACT, check Natalie’s robotics interest meeting date, and pick up more ibuprofen for my back.

metadata.title: Sunday night planning – truck, Mom, projects

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Attorney <cityattorney@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Cc: City Administrator
Date: January 23, 2025
Subject: Notice of claim re: Oak Street crash / Unit #24

I wanted to document what I’ve received so far regarding the Oak Street incident tied to Unit #24.

On January 22, 2025, I reviewed in my office at City Hall in Brainerd a notice of claim from attorney Mark Jensen with Jensen & Holt Law Firm. The claim is on behalf of a resident involved in the January 10, 2025 vehicle crash on Oak Street near 8th Avenue NE, Brainerd. The letter alleges that delayed plowing on Oak Street, caused by Unit #24’s electrical failure that morning, contributed to the crash.

The claim specifically references me by name as Public Works Director and questions whether I ignored prior maintenance recommendations related to Unit #24. For the record, we have multiple work orders on file documenting recurring hydraulic and electrical issues, and we have been tracking these as part of the 2026–2030 Capital Improvement Plan justification.

From my perspective, the core issue is that the current condition of Unit #24 has already exposed the City—and me personally—to potential liability. Replacing this truck is necessary to reduce the risk of similar incidents and claims. I will keep any reference to this notice of claim, the Oak/8th crash, or Jensen & Holt out of public agenda memos and stick to reliability, costs, and CIP alignment.

Please advise on any additional documentation you need from the Public Works Department.

Michael Robert Thompson
Public Works Director
City of Brainerd Public Works Department

metadata.subject: Notice of claim re: Oak Street crash / Unit #24

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Brian Thompson <brian.thompson@email.com>
Date: May 6, 2024
Subject: That plow accident I mentioned

Figured I’d give you the fuller story instead of the 30‑second version over the phone.

You remember the rear‑end collision I mentioned involving one of our plow trucks near Brainerd High School back on February 3, 2024? That was Truck #18. It happened right around dismissal time, and of course it turned out the other vehicle was driven by one of Evan’s classmates. The kid’s okay now, but there were some injuries.

The city’s insurer wrapped things up last week and settled the injury claim from the family for $47,500. The family’s attorney, working through Crow Wing Legal Aid, and the insurer both pushed the angle that the older braking system on Truck #18 contributed to the crash. Not a great look for the City of Brainerd Public Works Department, or for me as the guy responsible for the fleet.

I’ve got to thread a needle here. Internally, I’m documenting that the Truck #18 accident and the $47.5k payout are part of why I’m recommending we retire that unit. But in the formal “Recommended Action Motion” for Council, I have to keep it high‑level: motion to approve the purchase so we can retire Truck #18, which was involved in that Feb 3, 2024 rear‑end collision near the high school, without getting into the weeds about Evan’s classmate being the injured minor.

Anyway, just wanted you to hear it straight from me before it turns into coffee‑shop gossip.

Mike

From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Dave Larson <dlarson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: April 10, 2024
Subject: Draft language for new tandem purchase

Dave,

Sharing this off the record because you’ve been in the middle of the Unit #24 mess with me.

I’m finalizing the staff report and “Recommended Action Motion” for the new tandem‑axle plow. Finance wants a clear number, so I’m planning to phrase it as: motion to approve the purchase in the amount not to exceed $312,750.00. That gives us enough room for the spec we talked about and still keeps Council from choking on the price tag.

What I can’t put in the public packet is the HR angle. The City Administrator and HR gave me that informal performance warning dated April 8, 2024, citing “equipment reliability and route coverage” after Councilmember Harris’s March 31, 2024 complaint about unplowed sections of Maple Ridge Drive during that late‑season snowfall—including my own place at 714 Maple Ridge Drive, Brainerd, MN 56401. Having my street missed did not help my case.

Internally, I’m blunt: I need this purchase to show the City Administrator and HR that I’m addressing those concerns. Externally, the memo will just talk about aging equipment, reduced maintenance costs, and better coverage.

If you have any last‑minute feedback on the spec or think there’s a better way to frame the reliability issues without waving the HR flag, let me know before tomorrow afternoon.

Thanks,

Mike
Public Works Director
City of Brainerd

metadata.subject: Draft language for new tandem purchase
metadata.location: City of Brainerd Public Works Department

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mthompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Brainerd City Council; City Administrator <cadmin@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
Date: February 10, 2025, 8:12 PM
Subject: Recommended Action – Replacement of Public Works Truck #24

Council Members,

As you review the 2026–2030 Capital Improvement Plan items for Public Works, I want to provide additional context for the recommended replacement of Public Works Truck #24 (2009 International tandem-axle plow) ahead of next week’s work session. I’ll also acknowledge up front that this request is coming to you later in the budget cycle than it probably should have; I kept hoping the complaints about route delays and equipment downtime would taper off on their own and didn’t push to get this on an earlier agenda until a few of you started getting calls from residents.

Recommended Action Motion: Motion to approve the purchase as outlined so I can avoid a repeat of the January 15, 2025 incident on Highway 210 when Dave Larson and I had to call my cardiologist, Dr. Karen Ellingson at Essentia Health–Brainerd, from the cab of Truck #24 because my blood pressure spiked during a 14-hour plowing shift and I nearly blacked out while trying to keep the route open with a failing sander.

I recognize that is a very personal detail to share, but I feel it illustrates the operational and safety risks we are now facing. On January 15, we were short a driver due to illness, and Dave (our senior equipment operator) and I split the east side routes. The ongoing hydraulic and sander issues with #24 required constant manual intervention in whiteout conditions on Highway 210, which contributed to the health scare.

Beyond my own situation, this has implications for crew safety, route reliability, and liability for the City of Brainerd. Replacing #24 will reduce unplanned downtime, overtime costs, and the need to stretch remaining staff and equipment beyond safe limits during major events.

I’m available to discuss further prior to the meeting.

Michael Thompson
Public Works Director
City of Brainerd Public Works Department

metadata.subject: Recommended Action – Replacement of Public Works Truck #24
metadata.location: City of Brainerd Public Works Department

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <michael.thompson714@gmail.com>
To: Laura Thompson <laura.thompson@gmail.com>
Date: January 16, 2025, 6:41 AM
Subject: About last night on 210

Laura,

I didn’t want to dump all of this on you when I got home at 2 a.m., but I should explain what actually happened on Highway 210 last night. The storm was worse than forecast, and we were already down one operator, so I ended up back in Public Works Truck #24 with Dave Larson to cover the east side. By the time we hit hour 12 of what turned into a 14-hour plowing shift, the sander started acting up again and the hydraulics were cutting in and out.

Somewhere near the college entrance, my chest got tight and I felt lightheaded enough that Dave made me pull over. We sat there in the cab of Truck #24, flashers on in the middle of the storm, and he basically insisted we call my cardiologist, Dr. Karen Ellingson at Essentia Health–Brainerd. She talked us through checking my blood pressure with the portable cuff I keep in my bag and had me sit for a while before we finished the route.

I didn’t fully black out, but it was close, and it scared both of us more than I let on when I walked in the door. I know you and Dr. Ellingson have been telling me I can’t keep doing these marathon shifts, especially with my family history.

We’ll talk more tonight, but I wanted you to hear the whole story.

Love,
Michael

Personal Note – January 16, 2025
Location: Home office – 714 Maple Ridge Drive, Brainerd

Need to get this down while it’s fresh.

1. Health follow-up
   - Call Essentia Health–Brainerd cardiology clinic to schedule appointment with Dr. Karen Ellingson ASAP (tell scheduler about last night’s on-duty health scare with near blackout).
   - Ask her directly whether it’s medically acceptable for me to be out on 14-hour plowing shifts anymore, especially in something as unreliable as Public Works Truck #24.
   - Bring BP readings from the last month + details from January 15, 2025 on Highway 210 (time of symptoms, how long we were stopped, etc.).

2. Work implications
   - Talk to Dave Larson (senior equipment operator and close friend) about documenting what happened in the cab of Truck #24 so it’s not just my word in the record. We did make that phone call to Dr. Ellingson from the truck during the storm; that needs to be in the incident file.
   - Draft language for Council packet explaining why #24 is now a safety issue, not just a maintenance headache.

3. Family
   - Be honest with Laura about how close I felt to blacking out. She deserves the full story.
   - Think about how to balance being Public Works Director with being around for Evan, Natalie, and Mom after what happened to Dad.

metadata.title: Personal Note – Health + Work Follow-up
metadata.location: Home office – 714 Maple Ridge Drive, Brainerd
